  1. Biostatistics
  2. Part I. Statistical thinking and data foundations
  3. 1. The Statistical Landscape

1. The Statistical Landscape

Author

A. J. Smit

Last updated

2026/03/21

“Most people use statistics like a drunk man uses a lamppost; more for support than illumination.”

— Andrew Lang

1 Welcome

1.1 BCB744 Biostatistics

The central task in this module is to align the biological question, the data collected, and the statistical model. This part of BCB744 develops the practical reasoning required to move from a biological question to a defensible statistical analysis. You will learn how to summarise data, choose appropriate inferential tools, fit and evaluate models, and present results within a reproducible workflow.

Use these module pages for timetable, assessment, and module-wide context:

  • BCB744 Module Overview
  • Biostatistics Self-Assessment

2 Introduction

Statistics is not a set of disconnected tests. In biology and ecology, it is part of scientific reasoning from the moment a question is framed. We use it to describe variation, quantify uncertainty, compare groups, assess associations, and build models that connect biological processes to observations.

This section assumes working familiarity with R, basic data handling, and graphical interpretation. If needed, review the Introduction to R materials before proceeding.

The emphasis throughout is on method selection, assumption checking, model interpretation, and clear scientific communication. As the sequence develops, named tests are treated as parts of a broader analytical framework rather than as isolated techniques.

3 Statistics in the Scientific Process

This section describes how statistical analysis fits into scientific workflow.

A scientific investigation in biology usually moves through the following stages:

  1. observe a pattern or phenomenon;
  2. formulate a clear biological question;
  3. design a study that can produce relevant data;
  4. identify the response, predictors, and experimental units;
  5. anticipate alternative explanations and possible confounders;
  6. choose an analysis that matches the design and data structure;
  7. explore, analyse, and interpret the data; and
  8. communicate the results clearly in words, figures, and tables.

Statistics therefore begins long before the final analysis. If the design is weak, no later method can fully rescue the inference.

The analytical step in this workflow is formalised through statistical models. Some are simple and familiar, such as t-tests or ANOVA, while others are richer regression models that allow several predictors, non-normal responses, dependence, or nonlinear structure.

4 Key Concepts

This section defines the core ideas used throughout the module.

  • Statistical reasoning links questions, data, and models.
  • Inference depends on the alignment of design, analysis, and interpretation.
  • Models provide formal descriptions of how responses vary with predictors.
  • Explanation and prediction are related but distinct analytical goals.
  • Assumptions are part of the analysis, not an afterthought.

5 Core Principles

This section states the practical rules that should guide analysis throughout the module.

  • Define the biological question before choosing the method.
  • Understand how the data were generated and what the experimental units are.
  • Match the model to the process you are trying to explain.
  • Check assumptions explicitly and treat violations as meaningful information.
  • Distinguish between explanatory aims and predictive aims before interpreting coefficients or fit.

6 What This Section Will Help You Do

The biostatistics chapter sequence is designed to develop practical analytical competence. By the end of this section, you should be able to:

  • summarise and visualise biological data to reveal structure, variability, and anomalies;
  • choose appropriate statistical methods for common biological questions;
  • fit and interpret linear and generalised models;
  • evaluate model assumptions, identify violations, and explain their consequences for inference;
  • integrate statistical analysis into a reproducible scientific workflow;
  • communicate your methods and results clearly.

7 Section Structure

The material is organised into five parts, each addressing a distinct stage of statistical reasoning:

7.1 Statistical Thinking and Data Foundations

Develops the core concepts of data description, variation, and uncertainty.

7.2 Core Hypothesis Tests

Introduces formal inference for comparing groups and assessing associations.

7.3 Regression and Model Building

Moves from tests to models that explain variation in biological data.

7.4 Dependence and Model Extensions

Addresses non-independence, flexible model structures, and extended modelling frameworks.

7.5 Reproducible Practice

Connects statistical analysis to transparent and repeatable scientific workflows.

8 Recommended Study Sequence

If you are new to statistics, follow the parts in order. Students with prior training may use early chapters as review before focusing on inference and modelling.

8.1 Part I. Foundations

  • The Statistical Landscape
  • Summarising Biological Data
  • Visualising Data
  • Distributions, Sampling, and Uncertainty

8.2 Part II. Inference

  • Statistical Inference
  • Assumptions and Transformations
  • t-Tests
  • ANOVA
  • Correlation and Association
  • Choosing the Right Test

8.3 Part III. Modelling

  • Simple Linear Regression
  • Polynomial Regression
  • Multiple Regression and Model Specification
  • Interaction Effects
  • Collinearity, Confounding, and Measurement Error
  • Model Checking and Evaluation

8.4 Part IV. Extensions

  • Pseudoreplication
  • Dependence and Mixed Models
  • Generalised Linear Models
  • Generalised Additive Models
  • Nonlinear Regression
  • Quantile Regression
  • Prediction and Explanation
  • Regularisation

8.5 Part V. Reproducibility

  • Reproducible Workflow

Begin with The Statistical Landscape and progress sequentially. Return to earlier chapters as needed when concepts reappear in later modelling sections.

9 Assessment and Practice

Use the assessment materials alongside the chapter sequence:

9.1 Biostatistics Self-Assessment

Use this to check conceptual understanding after completing each major block.

9.2 Biostatistics Example 1

Use this to practise integrated analysis and interpretation across multiple concepts.

Consistent practice and reflection are essential for developing reliable statistical judgement.

Section overview
2. Summarising Biological Data
Source Code
---
title: "1. The Statistical Landscape"
author: "A. J. Smit"
date: last-modified
date-format: "YYYY/MM/DD"
reference-location: margin
---

```{r code-brewing-opts, echo=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  comment = "R>",
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE,
  fig.asp = NULL,
  fig.align = "center",
  fig.retina = 2,
  dpi = 300
)

ggplot2::theme_set(
  ggplot2::theme_grey(base_size = 8)
)
```

> *"Most people use statistics like a drunk man uses a lamppost; more for support than illumination."*
>
> --- Andrew Lang

# Welcome

## BCB744 Biostatistics

The central task in this module is to align the biological question, the data collected, and the statistical model. This part of BCB744 develops the practical reasoning required to move from a biological question to a defensible statistical analysis. You will learn how to summarise data, choose appropriate inferential tools, fit and evaluate models, and present results within a reproducible workflow.

Use these module pages for timetable, assessment, and module-wide context:

-   [BCB744 Module Overview](../BCB744_index.qmd)
-   [Biostatistics Self-Assessment](../assessments/BCB744_Biostatistics_Self-Assessment.qmd)

# Introduction

Statistics is not a set of disconnected tests. In biology and ecology, it is part of scientific reasoning from the moment a question is framed. We use it to describe variation, quantify uncertainty, compare groups, assess associations, and build models that connect biological processes to observations.

This section assumes working familiarity with R, basic data handling, and graphical interpretation. If needed, review the [Introduction to R](../intro_r/index.qmd) materials before proceeding.

The emphasis throughout is on method selection, assumption checking, model interpretation, and clear scientific communication. As the sequence develops, named tests are treated as parts of a broader analytical framework rather than as isolated techniques.

# Statistics in the Scientific Process

This section describes how statistical analysis fits into scientific workflow.

A scientific investigation in biology usually moves through the following stages:

1. observe a pattern or phenomenon;
2. formulate a clear biological question;
3. design a study that can produce relevant data;
4. identify the response, predictors, and experimental units;
5. anticipate alternative explanations and possible confounders;
6. choose an analysis that matches the design and data structure;
7. explore, analyse, and interpret the data; and
8. communicate the results clearly in words, figures, and tables.

Statistics therefore begins long before the final analysis. If the design is weak, no later method can fully rescue the inference.

The analytical step in this workflow is formalised through statistical models. Some are simple and familiar, such as `t`-tests or ANOVA, while others are richer regression models that allow several predictors, non-normal responses, dependence, or nonlinear structure.

# Key Concepts

This section defines the core ideas used throughout the module.

-   **Statistical reasoning** links questions, data, and models.
-   **Inference** depends on the alignment of design, analysis, and interpretation.
-   **Models** provide formal descriptions of how responses vary with predictors.
-   **Explanation and prediction** are related but distinct analytical goals.
-   **Assumptions** are part of the analysis, not an afterthought.

# Core Principles

This section states the practical rules that should guide analysis throughout the module.

-   Define the biological question before choosing the method.
-   Understand how the data were generated and what the experimental units are.
-   Match the model to the process you are trying to explain.
-   Check assumptions explicitly and treat violations as meaningful information.
-   Distinguish between explanatory aims and predictive aims before interpreting coefficients or fit.

# What This Section Will Help You Do

The biostatistics chapter sequence is designed to develop practical analytical competence. By the end of this section, you should be able to:

-   summarise and visualise biological data to reveal structure, variability, and anomalies;
-   choose appropriate statistical methods for common biological questions;
-   fit and interpret linear and generalised models;
-   evaluate model assumptions, identify violations, and explain their consequences for inference;
-   integrate statistical analysis into a reproducible scientific workflow;
-   communicate your methods and results clearly.

# Section Structure

The material is organised into five parts, each addressing a distinct stage of statistical reasoning:

## Statistical Thinking and Data Foundations

Develops the core concepts of data description, variation, and uncertainty.

## Core Hypothesis Tests

Introduces formal inference for comparing groups and assessing associations.

## Regression and Model Building

Moves from tests to models that explain variation in biological data.

## Dependence and Model Extensions

Addresses non-independence, flexible model structures, and extended modelling frameworks.

## Reproducible Practice

Connects statistical analysis to transparent and repeatable scientific workflows.

# Recommended Study Sequence

If you are new to statistics, follow the parts in order. Students with prior training may use early chapters as review before focusing on inference and modelling.

## Part I. Foundations

-   [The Statistical Landscape](01-statistical-landscape.qmd)
-   [Summarising Biological Data](02-summarise-and-describe.qmd)
-   [Visualising Data](03-visualise.qmd)
-   [Distributions, Sampling, and Uncertainty](04-distributions-sampling-uncertainty.qmd)

## Part II. Inference

-   [Statistical Inference](05-inference.qmd)
-   [Assumptions and Transformations](06-assumptions-and-transformations.qmd)
-   [*t*-Tests](07-t_tests.qmd)
-   [ANOVA](08-anova.qmd)
-   [Correlation and Association](09-correlation-and-association.qmd)
-   [Choosing the Right Test](10-test-selection.qmd)

## Part III. Modelling

-   [Simple Linear Regression](11-simple-linear-regression.qmd)
-   [Polynomial Regression](12-polynomial-regression.qmd)
-   [Multiple Regression and Model Specification](13-multiple-regression-and-model-specification.qmd)
-   [Interaction Effects](14-interaction-effects.qmd)
-   [Collinearity, Confounding, and Measurement Error](15-collinearity-confounding-measurement-error.qmd)
-   [Model Checking and Evaluation](16-model-checking-and-evaluation.qmd)

## Part IV. Extensions

-   [Pseudoreplication](17-pseudoreplication.qmd)
-   [Dependence and Mixed Models](18-dependence-and-mixed-models.qmd)
-   [Generalised Linear Models](19-generalised-linear-models.qmd)
-   [Generalised Additive Models](20-generalised-additive-models.qmd)
-   [Nonlinear Regression](21-nonlinear-regression.qmd)
-   [Quantile Regression](22-quantile-regression.qmd)
-   [Prediction and Explanation](23-prediction-and-explanation.qmd)
-   [Regularisation](24-regularisation.qmd)

## Part V. Reproducibility

-   [Reproducible Workflow](25-reproducible-workflow.qmd)

Begin with [The Statistical Landscape](01-statistical-landscape.qmd) and progress sequentially. Return to earlier chapters as needed when concepts reappear in later modelling sections.

# Assessment and Practice

Use the assessment materials alongside the chapter sequence:

## [Biostatistics Self-Assessment](../assessments/BCB744_Biostatistics_Self-Assessment.qmd)

Use this to check conceptual understanding after completing each major block.

## [Biostatistics Example 1](../assessments/BCB744_BioStats_Example_1.qmd)

Use this to practise integrated analysis and interpretation across multiple concepts.

Consistent practice and reflection are essential for developing reliable statistical judgement.
